title: IT-to-OT Perimeter Firewall Rule Violation
spl:
index=ot sourcetype="ics:firewall"
| where (src_zone="IT" AND dst_zone="OT")
| lookup ot_perimeter_allow_rules rule_id OUTPUT allowed_description
| where isnull(allowed_description) AND action="permit"
| stats count earliest(_time) as first_seen values(dest_port) as ports by src, dest, rule_id
| sort - count
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest | sort - count
description: Detects traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary that violates approved rules, preserving segmentation and reducing ransomware lateral movement into control systems.
value: Detects traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary that violates approved rules, preserving segmentation and reducing ransomware lateral movement into control systems.
implementation: Consume normalized perimeter logs in Splunk ES. Reconcile rule_id with change-managed policies. Use for continuous compliance evidence.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot` `sourcetype="ics:firewall"` with `rule_id`, `action`, `src_zone`, `dst_zone`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Consume normalized perimeter logs in Splunk ES. Reconcile rule_id with change-managed policies. Use for continuous compliance evidence.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot sourcetype="ics:firewall"
| where (src_zone="IT" AND dst_zone="OT")
| lookup ot_perimeter_allow_rules rule_id OUTPUT allowed_description
| where isnull(allowed_description) AND action="permit"
| stats count earliest(_time) as first_seen values(dest_port) as ports by src, dest, rule_id
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**IT-to-OT Perimeter Firewall Rule Violation** ‚Äî Detects traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary that violates approved rules, preserving segmentation and reducing ransomware lateral movement into control systems.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="ics:firewall"` with `rule_id`, `action`, `src_zone`, `dst_zone`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot; **sourcetype**: ics:firewall. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=ot, sourcetype="ics:firewall". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where (src_zone="IT" AND dst_zone="OT")` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where isnull(allowed_description) AND action="permit"` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src, dest, rule_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**IT-to-OT Perimeter Firewall Rule Violation** ‚Äî Detects traffic crossing the IT/OT boundary that violates approved rules, preserving segmentation and reducing ransomware lateral movement into control systems.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot` `sourcetype="ics:firewall"` with `rule_id`, `action`, `src_zone`, `dst_zone`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): OT Security Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 5151). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Sankey IT‚ÜíOT flows, Table of violating rules, Trend of unique new flows.
visualization: Sankey IT‚ÜíOT flows, Table of violating rules, Trend of unique new flows.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
